institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College, tutor + Clerk of the Faculty, 1808-1812 (resigned to marry Elisabeth Daggett).
South Carolina College, tutor c. 1807-1808 (concurrent with private law study under brother John Hooker, practicing lawyer in Columbia SC).

notable.notable_blurb: Old Red College school precursor to Miss Porter's

narrative_blocks.header: Old Red College became Miss Porter's first schoolhouse
narrative_blocks.body: He converted the old Hooker family house — formerly home of a descendant of Ezekiel Cheever, founder of Boston Latin — into the 'Old Red College' preparatory school in Farmington. The building was later demolished to make way for Miss Porter's School's first schoolhouse.

narrative_blocks.header: His wife's uncle signed the Declaration of Independence
narrative_blocks.body: While serving as Yale tutor and Clerk of the Faculty (1808-1812), he met Elisabeth Daggett — niece of Roger Sherman, the only Founding Father to sign all four great state papers. He resigned his Yale post in 1812 to marry her.

narrative_blocks.header: His son compiled this very Hooker genealogy
narrative_blocks.body: His son Edward, born Christmas Day 1822, became Commander Edward Hooker U.S.N. In retirement he compiled the foundational 1909 Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker — the primary genealogical source for this entire project.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale tutor who studied law in South Carolina
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale 1805 and traveled to Columbia, South Carolina to study law with his elder brother John, also teaching briefly at Old Ninety Six. Yale appointed him tutor in 1808 — a post he held until 1812 when he returned to Farmington.

narrative_blocks.header: Painted by Jared Bradley Flagg around 1840
narrative_blocks.body: His c. 1840 portrait by Jared Bradley Flagg, oil on canvas 30 x 24.5 inches, descended through his great-grandson Joseph K. Hooker. It now hangs at the Harriet Beecher Stowe Center in Hartford.

cross_connections.display_label: Old Red College building site became Miss Porter's first schoolhouse

career.organization: Cambridge SC (Old Ninety Six), South Carolina
career.notes: Brief teaching stint while studying law in South Carolina with brother John.

career.organization: Under brother John Hooker, practicing lawyer at Columbia, South Carolina
career.notes: Edward did not complete legal practice — abandoned law for academia and farming when he returned to CT in 1808.
Concurrent with continuing law study under his brother.
Met Elisabeth Daggett (niece of Roger Sherman, Declaration of Independence signer) through Yale connections; resigned tutorship to marry her.
Returned to Farmington 1812 to manage father's farm and care for father in declining years. Built home for Elisabeth on family land.

career.role: Founder + Master, "Old Red College" private school
career.organization: Farmington CT (in family's former Cheever-descendant house)
career.notes: After his mother's death, Edward brought his father into his own house and converted the old family home into a private collegiate-prep school. The 'Old Red College' fitted many future public figures for college. The house had been previously inhabited by a descendant of Ezekiel Cheever (founder of Boston Latin School and author of the first American school books). Edward later proposed closing his school in favor of a new Farmington Academy under Simeon Hart. The school closed in 1816 when the town opened its own academy in the village center under Simeon Hart.

career.role: Local public affairs + literary work + scientific farming
career.notes: After closing the school, Edward devoted himself to writing, town affairs, and scientific farming until his death.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Owner (1811-1846); 1811 Federal-style house built for him on High Street, Farmington CT. Inherited at his death by son John Hooker (H01626).

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College, tutor + Clerk of the Faculty, 1808-1812 (resigned to marry Elisabeth Daggett).
South Carolina College, tutor c. 1807-1808 (concurrent with private law study under brother John Hooker, practicing lawyer in Columbia SC).

narrative_blocks.body: His c. 1840 portrait by Jared Bradley Flagg, oil on canvas 30 x 24.5 inches, descended through his great-grandson Joseph K. Hooker. It now hangs at the Harriet Beecher Stowe Center in Hartford.

career.notes: Brief teaching stint while studying law in South Carolina with brother John.

Concurrent with continuing law study under his brother.
Met Elisabeth Daggett (niece of Roger Sherman, Declaration of Independence signer) through Yale connections; resigned tutorship to marry her.
Returned to Farmington 1812 to manage father's farm and care for father in declining years. Built home for Elisabeth on family land.

career.role: Founder + Master, "Old Red College" private school
career.organization: Farmington CT (in family's former Cheever-descendant house)
career.notes: After his mother's death, Edward brought his father into his own house and converted the old family home into a private collegiate-prep school. The 'Old Red College' fitted many future public figures for college. The house had been previously inhabited by a descendant of Ezekiel Cheever (founder of Boston Latin School and author of the first American school books). Edward later proposed closing his school in favor of a new Farmington Academy under Simeon Hart. The school closed in 1816 when the town opened its own academy in the village center under Simeon Hart.

career.role: Local public affairs + literary work + scientific farming
career.notes: After closing the school, Edward devoted himself to writing, town affairs, and scientific farming until his death.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Owner (1811-1846); 1811 Federal-style house built for him on High Street, Farmington CT. Inherited at his death by son John Hooker (H01626).

parents.father_research_notes: Henry Daggett Esq (b. April 9, 1741 Attleboro MA; d. Sept 24, 1830 Attleboro MA, age 89). Per WikiTree: father Elihu Daggett, mother Rebecca Stanley Daggett. Grandparents: Mayhew Daggett (~1686-1752 Tisbury Martha's Vineyard MA) + Joanna Bevin (b. Feb 10, 1687 Glastonbury Hartford Co CT). The Tisbury / Martha's Vineyard connection ties Henry's Daggett lineage to the Edgartown MA Daggett family. Henry was COUSIN (not brother) of Rev. Naphtali Daggett D.D. (1727-1780), Yale College President 1766-1777 — the FIRST Yale graduate to head the institution. Naphtali's grandparents were John Daggett (1662-1724 Edgartown) + Sarah Pease (1661-1735 Edgartown Dukes Co MA) — different grandparents from Henry but both lineages trace to the Watertown MA / Edgartown MA Daggett family of John Daggett (immigrant ~1630s, ancestor of Thomas Daggett 1630-1691 of Edgartown). Henry Daggett Esq + Naphtali Daggett were thus 2nd or 3rd cousins, both descending from the same MA Puritan Daggett family via different sons of Thomas Daggett of Edgartown.
parents.mother_research_notes: Elisabeth Prescott (b. Dec 1, 1752; d. May 11, 1813 Danvers MA, age 60). Sister of Rebecca Prescott who married Roger Sherman (Signer of the Declaration of Independence) in Danvers MA on May 12, 1763 — making Roger Sherman uncle-by-marriage to Elisabeth Daggett. Other Prescott siblings: Martha (Goodhue), James, Mercy (Gibbs), Benjamin Daggett Prescott, Mary (Barrett).

narrative_blocks.header: Niece of Declaration signer Roger Sherman
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother's sister Rebecca Prescott married Roger Sherman in Danvers in 1763. Sherman signed both the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father's cousin was Yale's wartime president
narrative_blocks.body: Naphtali Daggett served as president pro tempore of Yale from 1766 to 1777. During the 1779 British raid on New Haven he took up a shotgun in the town's defense and was captured by the invaders.

narrative_blocks.header: She married a Yale-educated senator who co-founded Nook
narrative_blocks.body: Elisabeth Daggett Hooker married Francis Gillette in 1834 — a Yale valedictorian who later became a U.S. Senator and co-founded Hartford's Nook Farm community with her brother John. Every one of their five children who survived infancy carried 'Hooker' as a middle name: Ashbell Frank, Elisabeth Hooker, Edward Hooker, Robert Hooker, and William Hooker.

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College 1829; valedictorian elected unanimously by classmates.

narrative_blocks.header: He went from valedictorian to the Senate
narrative_blocks.body: Gillette was elected valedictorian of Yale's class of 1829, then served in the Connecticut legislature before reaching Washington. He filled a United States Senate vacancy as a Free Soil man in 1854, in the thick of the slavery fight.

narrative_blocks.header: His farmhouse sheltered the Underground Railroad
narrative_blocks.body: Gillette ran an Underground Railroad station at his Bloomfield farm and helped found Connecticut's Republican Party. He was a silent partner in the Evening Press, the state's first Republican newspaper.

narrative_blocks.header: He built Nook Farm with John Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: In 1853 Gillette and his brother-in-law John Hooker founded Nook Farm, the Hartford enclave that would draw Mark Twain and Harriet Beecher Stowe. His wife was Elisabeth Daggett Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: His son became Sherlock Holmes on stage
narrative_blocks.body: Gillette's son William Hooker Gillette grew into the celebrated actor who defined Sherlock Holmes for American audiences. Another son, Edward, went to Congress.

cross_connections.display_label: co-founded Nook Farm, drawing Twain and Stowe there

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Co-founder (from 1853); Co-founded Nook Farm with brother-in-law John Hooker.

burial.plot_notes: Originally buried New Helvetia Cemetery Sacramento CA; remains removed by brother Robert to Riverside Cemetery Farmington CT

narrative_blocks.header: Died of consumption in Sacramento at 23
narrative_blocks.body: Frank Ashbel Gillette moved to California and died in Sacramento on August 4, 1859, at age 23, of dyspeptic consumption. He was originally buried in New Helvetia Cemetery, Sacramento. His brother Robert — later killed at Fort Fisher — had his remains removed and brought back to the Hooker family plot at Riverside Cemetery in Farmington, Connecticut.

cross_connections.display_label: Her husband's brother Charles co-wrote The Gilded Age with Twain at Nook Farm in 1873 — she and George lived in the same community.

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband's brother co-wrote The Gilded Age
narrative_blocks.body: Elisabeth Hooker Gillette married George Henry Warner in 1864 and moved to Hartford's Nook Farm community — where her brother John Hooker and his wife Isabella Beecher were already established. When Mark Twain arrived in 1874, George's brother Charles Dudley Warner was already working at the Hartford Courant. In 1873, after their wives challenged them to write a better novel, Twain and Charles Warner co-wrote The Gilded Age — which named an era.

narrative_blocks.header: He ran for Congress with no money
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Hooker Gillette moved to Iowa in 1863 to manage land his father, Senator Francis Gillette, and uncle John Hooker had bought there. In 1878 he ran for Congress on the Greenback ticket in a district with a six-thousand-vote Republican majority, funding the campaign by selling newspapers from the platform at ten cents each. He won.

narrative_blocks.header: He led the Greenback Party's national committee
narrative_blocks.body: Beyond his single term in Congress, Gillette chaired the Greenback Party's national committee and edited the Iowa Tribune for a decade. He later ran, without success, for Iowa secretary of state on the People's Party ticket.

cross_connections.display_label: His uncle John Hooker co-owned the Iowa land that sent Edward west in 1863 — his father and Hooker had bought thousands of Iowa acres together.
shares the 1881 Greenback-Labor Advocates print with him

death.cause: Killed at the capture of Fort Fisher — explosion of the magazine

sources.label: Hartford obituary — Death of Paymaster Gillette, 1865
NY State Military Museum — CDV photograph collection

narrative_blocks.header: Killed the morning after Fort Fisher surrendered
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Hooker Gillette was paymaster aboard the U.S.S. Gettysburg during both assaults on Fort Fisher. On the morning of January 16, 1865 — after the battle was over and the fort had surrendered — he went in with others to inspect the damage. Drunk Federal soldiers, sailors, and marines, carrying torches and firing weapons, accidentally ignited the Confederate powder magazine.

narrative_blocks.header: He had traveled to China and California before
narrative_blocks.body: Before the war, Robert had traveled to China, California and other distant fields before turning 18. He helped recruit two companies of men, then joined the 14th Connecticut as a Captain — but a fever from exposure at Harpers Ferry shattered his health and he resigned his commission. In 1863 he was appointed assistant paymaster in the Navy, serving aboard blockade ships along the North Carolina coast.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in infancy — year unknown
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Hooker Gillette was born June 24, 1845 in Bloomfield, Connecticut. EH 1909 records her death as August 27 of an unspecified year — most likely within days or weeks of her birth. The year in the EH text is cut off.

notable.notable_blurb: Defined Sherlock Holmes on stage; built famed castle

cross_connections.display_label: Twain got him his first acting job at the Globe, 1875

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Built Gillette Castle 1914-1919 as his home; designed it himself including 47 unique locks, secret passages, and a miniature railway.

institutions.institution_blurb: No formal Yale degree; educated Hartford High School, College of the City of New York, and Boston University.

narrative_blocks.header: Defined Sherlock Holmes on stage for thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: He performed Sherlock Holmes more than 1,300 times across more than thirty years, in his own 1899 stage adaptation written with Conan Doyle's blessing. The play established Holmes's deerstalker silhouette and 'Elementary, my dear Watson' as cultural shorthand.

narrative_blocks.header: Invented Holmes's iconic curved calabash pipe
narrative_blocks.body: Sidney Paget's straight pipe from the Strand illustrations was impractical on stage, so he introduced the curved calabash. He needed to speak his lines while smoking, and the curved stem freed his mouth — the pipe became Holmes's most enduring visual signature.

narrative_blocks.header: Built a 24-room castle above the Connecticut River
narrative_blocks.body: He designed every detail of his East Haddam fieldstone castle himself between 1914 and 1919 — 47 unique trick-locked doors, secret passages, mirrors above the great hall to spy on arriving guests, and a spring-loaded bar. It is now Gillette Castle State Park.

notable.notable_blurb: Connecticut abolitionist who bought Pennington's freedom

cross_connections.display_label: Practiced law where Sarah Porter first taught her students
cross_connections.description: Hooker practiced law in the stone store at 96 Main Street Farmington in 1843, the same building where Sarah Porter taught her first students as she established her school.

cross_connections.display_label: Co-founded Nook Farm; Mark Twain moved in 1874
cross_connections.description: John Hooker helped found the Nook Farm community on Farmington Avenue Hartford; Mark Twain moved in as neighbor in 1874.

cross_connections.display_label: His granddaughter preserved the Nook Farm community
cross_connections.description: Katharine Seymour Day was John Hooker's granddaughter — she grew up on Nook Farm, which he had co-founded, and spent her life preserving the community he created.

cross_connections.display_label: co-founded Nook Farm and sat in the US Senate
cross_connections.description: Co-founded Nook Farm together in 1853 — Hooker and Gillette bought 140 Hartford acres and built the literary community.

cross_connections.display_label: Iowa land they bought together launched Gillette's career
cross_connections.description: Gillette went to Iowa to manage land Hooker and his own father had purchased together — a Hartford investment that launched a Greenback congressman.

cross_connections.display_label: Bought Pennington's freedom from slavery in 1851
cross_connections.description: When the 1850 Fugitive Slave Law endangered Pennington — his friend and parishioner — Hooker opened secret negotiations with Frisby Tilghman's heirs. Hooker briefly took legal 'ownership' of Pennington to manumit him in New York; British abolitionists raised the purchase funds.

cross_connections.display_label: Married daughter of Rev. Lyman Beecher, recruited by this jurist.
Fellow Connecticut abolitionist working through law and Underground Railroad.
Fellow Hooker-descendant Connecticut abolitionist of the same generation.
Fellow Hooker-descendant abolitionist of the antebellum Northern circuit.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-founded Nook Farm; invited Mark Twain next door
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker was a Hartford lawyer who, with Francis Gillette, co-purchased the Nook Farm land in 1853 and developed it into the literary colony where his wife Isabella Beecher Hooker, Harriet Beecher Stowe, and eventually Mark Twain lived as neighbors. He was also a supporter of women's suffrage, notably influenced by watching his wife Isabella dedicate her life to it after reading Blackstone in his law office.

narrative_blocks.header: Bought Rev. Pennington's freedom from slavery, 1851
narrative_blocks.body: After the 1850 Fugitive Slave Law endangered formerly-enslaved people living in the North, John Hooker legally purchased Rev. James Pennington's freedom from his former owner's estate in 1851. Pennington had escaped Maryland slavery, attended Yale, become a Congregational minister, and authored 'The Fugitive Blacksmith' (1849).

narrative_blocks.header: Published the Charter Oak abolitionist newspaper, 1838
narrative_blocks.body: John Hooker published the Charter Oak with the Connecticut Anti-Slavery Society of Hartford. Its motto: 'Free Principles — Free Men — Free Speech — And A Free Press.' Digital copies survive at the Connecticut State Library's Newspapers of Connecticut collection.

narrative_blocks.header: Co-wrote Connecticut's Married Women's Property Act
narrative_blocks.body: John and Isabella jointly wrote 'A Woman's Property Bill,' which became Connecticut's Married Women's Property Act of 1877. The act let a married woman control her own property and sue or be sued the same as her husband — undoing the coverture rules John had read about in Blackstone.

narrative_blocks.header: Reporter of Connecticut Supreme Court thirty-six years
narrative_blocks.body: In 1858 John Hooker became Reporter of the Connecticut Supreme Court of Errors — a role he held for thirty-six years until his 1894 retirement. He reported thirty-eight volumes of Connecticut Reports, the lasting professional achievement that defined his second career. The only longer-serving Connecticut reporter was Thomas Day (fifty-one years, 1802-1853), who had reported twenty-six volumes.

career.role: Lawyer + Clerk of the Connecticut Supreme Court
career.notes: Practicing lawyer in Hartford; long-tenured Clerk of the CT Supreme Court.

John and Isabella moved from Farmington to Hartford in early 1850s and helped establish the Nook Farm community with the Gillettes (his sister Elisabeth Hooker Gillette and her husband Francis Gillette). Mark Twain became a Nook Farm neighbor in 1874.

career.organization: Deming's Store, Mill Lane, Farmington CT
career.notes: John rented an office at Deming's Store directly next door to a room used by the Africans from the Amistad during their stay in Farmington.

career.role: Legal purchase of Rev. James Pennington's freedom
career.notes: After the 1850 Fugitive Slave Law, John legally purchased Rev. James Pennington's freedom from his former owner's estate. Pennington — formerly enslaved in Maryland, escaped to Connecticut, attended Yale, and became a Congregational minister — wrote 'The Fugitive Blacksmith' (1849).

career.role: CT women's property rights legislative campaign
career.notes: Influenced by his wife Isabella, John presented a bill making husbands and wives equal in property rights; passed Connecticut General Assembly 1877.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Inherited owner (1846-1864); Inherited from his father Edward Hooker (H00630) in 1846. John and Isabella lived here until early 1850s when they moved to Hartford to establish Nook Farm. House remained in family until sold 1864.
Co-founder (1853-1901); Co-founded with brother-in-law Francis Gillette (140 acres, Farmington Avenue).

parents.mother_research_notes: Harriet Porter Beecher (2nd wife of Lyman)

cross_connections.display_label: Half-sister of Stowe through Lyman Beecher's two wives
cross_connections.description: Half-sisters by the same father Lyman Beecher — Isabella by his second wife Harriet Porter, Harriet by his first wife Roxanna Foote. Isabella married into the Hooker family; Harriet wrote Uncle Tom's Cabin.

cross_connections.display_label: Nook Farm neighbor — community her family helped build
cross_connections.description: Isabella Beecher Hooker and Mark Twain were Nook Farm neighbors — she and John Hooker had helped establish the community on Farmington Avenue before Twain moved in 1874.

cross_connections.display_label: Half-siblings through Lyman Beecher; both reformers
cross_connections.description: Half-siblings through Lyman Beecher — Edward by Roxanna Foote, Isabella by Harriet Porter. Both reformers; Edward organized Illinois' first anti-slavery society.

cross_connections.display_label: Granddaughter who preserved her Nook Farm legacy
cross_connections.description: Katharine Seymour Day was Isabella's granddaughter — she grew up on Nook Farm surrounded by Isabella's suffragist work and spent her life preserving the community her grandmother helped build.

cross_connections.display_label: Ohio's leading suffragist abolitionist — Hooker descendant
was a later Maryland suffrage leader who also married a Hooker
led New York woman suffrage alongside her
a fellow abolitionist and suffrage orator of the same era

narrative_blocks.header: Read Blackstone — spent decades undoing it
narrative_blocks.body: At nineteen Isabella Beecher married John Hooker and became a regular visitor to his Hartford law office, where she read Blackstone's Commentaries and discovered that under common law marriage suspended a woman's legal standing entirely. She spent the rest of her life undoing it. She died in 1907 at eighty-four, still at work.

narrative_blocks.header: Seven-year fight that won married women property
narrative_blocks.body: Beginning in 1870 Isabella shepherded a married women's property bill through the Connecticut legislature year after year, defeated and reintroduced. It finally passed in 1877, ending the doctrine that a wife's earnings and property automatically belonged to her husband.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Connecticut's first suffrage association in 1869
narrative_blocks.body: Isabella founded the Connecticut Woman Suffrage Association in 1869, the state's first organized suffrage body. Two years later she paid out of her own pocket for a national suffrage convention in Washington, D.C.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Co-founder (1853-1907); Co-founded the community with husband John Hooker; raised 3 children at Forest + Hawthorn.

notable.primary_url_label: Naval History and Heritage Command — Manuscript Collection

narrative_blocks.header: Compiled this genealogy until he went blind
narrative_blocks.body: He compiled the foundational 1909 'Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker, Hartford Connecticut, 1586-1908' over many years; his eyesight failed before the work was complete. The 1909 volume is the foundational primary source for this entire project — the work that made every later genealogy of the Hooker line possible.

narrative_blocks.header: Wounded three times, twice promoted for gallantry
narrative_blocks.body: He entered Navy service 1861 as acting master (volunteer); was the first acting master wounded in the Civil War; wounded twice more during the conflict; was twice promoted for gallantry. Transferred to regular Navy; retired Commander December 1884.

narrative_blocks.header: Commanded a ship at twenty-three
narrative_blocks.body: He took his first ship command at twenty-three — well before his Civil War volunteer service. The early experience prepared him for the 1861 enlistment as acting master in the U.S. Navy when Lincoln's volunteer force was first called.

narrative_blocks.header: Nephew of Declaration signer Roger Sherman
narrative_blocks.body: His mother Elisabeth Daggett was a niece of Roger Sherman of Connecticut — signer of both the Declaration of Independence and the U.S. Constitution. The 1903 Brooklyn Eagle obituary erroneously called Sherman 'Robert' rather than Roger — a common period misprint.

cross_connections.display_label: Talcott compiled the parallel 1876 Talcott Genealogy book
Compiled 1909 Hooker parallel to Whittlesey Memorials co-author
Compiled 1909 Hooker parallel to Whittlesey's 1855 history
Great-uncle: Declaration of Independence and U.S. Constitution signer
Wrote 1874 Dwight and 1871 Strong family genealogies

H00288.prominent_name_reason: Col. Noadiah Hooker — Revolutionary War officer who raised the first Farmington troops and commanded a regiment at Burgoyne's surrender (Saratoga, 1777); 40 years Justice of the Peace; Farmington representative many sessions. "In all relations of life, both public and private, he discharged every duty with credit to himself and benefit to his fellow citizens."

cross_connections.display_label: Bull engraved the Farmington Library bookplate; both on founding committee.
First cousins who served together — Roger enlisted in his company.
Friend-compatriots in the Revolutionary War.
Norton served under his command at Peekskill in spring 1777.

notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary War Colonel; 40 years Justice of Peace

narrative_blocks.header: Commanded a regiment at Burgoyne's surrender
narrative_blocks.body: His Connecticut regiment fought in the Saratoga Campaign of 1777, where John Burgoyne's British army laid down its arms. The victory turned the war and brought France in on the American side.

narrative_blocks.header: First Farmington troops to reach Boston
narrative_blocks.body: He raised a hundred-man company of Farmington's elite and arrived at Roxbury three weeks before any other Connecticut soldiers. The Colonial Assembly commissioned him captain on arrival.

narrative_blocks.header: Sent an Oneida boy to mission school
narrative_blocks.body: In 1819 he paid for the education of eighteen-year-old Peter Augustus Takonotas at the Cornwall Foreign Mission School. The boy later took Hooker's surname.

narrative_blocks.header: Justice of the Peace for forty years
narrative_blocks.body: After the war he served as Farmington's Justice of the Peace from the 1780s through the 1820s. The role made him the local arbiter of debt, marriage, and civil disputes for two generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Late returning a library book in 1795
narrative_blocks.body: The Farmington Monthly Library, founded that year, recorded 'Col. Noadiah Hooker his shilling' for a late-return fine. Julius Gay's nineteenth-century history pamphlets preserved the entry.

education.school_name: No college noted — rose through militia ranks

military_service.notes: Raised the first troops enlisted at Farmington and marched in command of a company of 100+ men — the very elite of the vicinity. Arrived in Boston (Roxbury) three weeks before any other enlisted men from Connecticut. Commissioned Captain by Colonial Assembly. Commissioned Colonel by State Legislature 1776. Served in several regiments until close of War. Had command of a regiment at the capture of Burgoyne's army (Saratoga, 1777). Facebook genealogy research (2024) confirms: Samuel Woodruff (1734-1816) of Farmington CT served as private in Capt. Jesse Curtiss' company, Col. Noadiah Hooker's regiment, 1777. Also confirmed by Orderly Book of Capt. Ichabod Norton (1777, Peekskill): Norton served "under Colonel Hooker" at Peekskill 1777. Norton was husband of Ruth Strong (H00297), daughter of Ruth Hooker Strong — a Hooker descendant herself. Two branches of the Hooker tree serving together.
military_service.unit: Col. Noadiah Hooker's Regiment, Connecticut Militia

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery (Ancient Burying Ground), Farmington, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: Farmington militia captain his entire life
narrative_blocks.body: He held the rank of Captain in the Connecticut militia and was known by that title for the rest of his life. He spent his entire life in Farmington — born there February 15, 1704/5 and dying there December 19, 1764, in his 60th year.

narrative_blocks.header: The big chair — militia saluted him sitting
narrative_blocks.body: In later years he became so corpulent he could not attend to business. On fine days a large chair was placed in the front yard near the door, where he sat smoking his pipe and visiting with passersby. When the local militia paraded, it was customary for them to march around by his house and salute him in the big chair.

narrative_blocks.header: Heir to the famous Farmington Hooker Elm
narrative_blocks.body: He was the son of Hon. John Hooker — the Farmington judge whose famous Hooker Elm gave the town its hospitality tradition. His grandfather Rev. Samuel Hooker had ministered Farmington's church for thirty years; his great-great-grandfather Rev. Thomas Hooker founded Hartford.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.

INST089.name: South Carolina College
INST089.notes: Chartered 1801 as South Carolina College; now the University of South Carolina. Edward Hooker (H00630) served as a tutor here ~1807-1808 while concurrently studying law privately with his elder brother John Hooker (a practicing lawyer in Columbia SC). Edward also taught briefly at Cambridge SC (Old Ninety Six) before joining the College faculty.

crossConnections.display_label: Old Red College building site became Miss Porter's first schoolhouse
